__data__.text:
For the RecipeOrganizer project each recipe is expected to follow a simple, consistent schema:

Name – the title of the dish.
Ingredients – an array/list where each entry includes:
the ingredient’s name, and
the required quantity (e.g. “2 cups flour”, “1 tbsp sugar”).
Preparation instructions – a step‑by‑step narrative describing how to make the recipe.
Preparation time – the total time (e.g. “30 minutes”, “1 hour”) it takes to prepare the dish.
You can think of a recipe object in code roughly like:
{
    "name": "Chocolate Cake",
    "ingredients": [
        {"item": "flour", "quantity": "2 cups"},
        {"item": "sugar", "quantity": "1 cup"},
        …
    ],
    "instructions": "Preheat oven… mix ingredients… bake for 30 minutes.",
    "prep_time": "45 minutes"
}
This structure ensures every recipe holds the essential data needed by the organizer.

Name search
• User types a word or phrase.
• The system looks through each recipe’s name field and returns any recipe where the term appears (partial‑text matching).

Tag search
• User selects or enters a tag (e.g. cake, meat, quick).
• All recipes associated with that tag are returned.

Ingredient search
• User requests a particular ingredient (e.g. flour, egg).
• The system scans the ingredients list of every recipe.
• Any recipe containing that ingredient (matching the item field) is included in the results.

Queries can be combined (name + tag, ingredient + tag, etc.) to further narrow results.

This logic ensures fast, intuitive filtering: users get the matching set of recipes based on the keywords or tags they enter.

In RecipeOrganizer the “tagging system” is nothing more than a flexible way to classify and query recipes across whatever sub‑projects you’re working on (meat, fish, cakes, etc.).

-Tags are arbitrary labels attached to a recipe – e.g. meat, fish, cake, salad, vegan, quick‑prep, etc.
-Each recipe will be in the place that best describes it, such as "chicken soup" in "soups."
-The various “projects” you mentioned (Meat, Fish, Cake, Cheesecake, Pastries, Salad, Soup, Dairy, Ice Cream, Add‑ons, Pies, Snacks) simply represent common tag sets or collections of recipes that share a theme.
-When you build the organizer UI or query engine, you filter by one or more tags to pull the appropriate recipes for that project.
-Tags are stored alongside the other recipe fields (name, ingredients, instructions, prep time) so they’re part of the recipe’s data model and can be updated easily.

In short: assign descriptive tags to each recipe and then use those tags to group recipes into “Meat”, “Fish”, “Cake”, etc., collections without duplicating content.
